questions.to_find: The language generated by the given context-free grammar.

understand.plain: We have a grammar with three productions. S generates X followed by Y. X generates one or more a's (at least one a). Y generates equal numbers of a's and b's (i.e., a^k b^k for k >= 0, because Y -> aYb | epsilon). So S generates a string of a's (from X, at least 1) concatenated with a^k b^k (from Y, k >= 0). The total number of a's is at least 1 + k and number of b's is exactly k, so m >= n with n >= 0.

keywords.explain: A formal grammar where each production rule replaces a single non-terminal with a string of terminals and non-terminals.
keywords.example: S -> aSb | epsilon generates {a^n b^n | n >= 0}.

keywords.explain: Applying grammar rules step-by-step to derive terminal strings from the start symbol.
keywords.example: S -> XY -> aY -> a(aYb) -> aaepsilonb = aab, where X gives a and Y gives ab.

understand.visual_alt: The grammar S->XY, X->aX|a, Y->aYb|epsilon. X generates a^m for m>=1. Y generates a^n b^n for n>=0. Combined: a^m a^n b^n = a^(m+n) b^n where m>=1, n>=0, so total a-count >= n+1 > n, meaning a-count > b-count which means a-count >= b-count with strict possibility.

given.aim: Find the set of all strings derivable from S using the given grammar.

terms.meaning: X generates one or more a's: {a^m | m >= 1}
terms.example: X => a, X => aX => aa, X => aaa, ...
terms.connects: X contributes at least one a to the final string.

terms.meaning: Y generates equal numbers of a's and b's: {a^n b^n | n >= 0}
terms.example: Y => epsilon, Y => aYb => ab, Y => aYb => a(aYb)b => aabb
terms.connects: Y contributes n a's and n b's for some n >= 0.

terms.meaning: S generates a string from X followed by a string from Y
terms.example: S => XY => a^m . a^n b^n = a^(m+n) b^n
terms.connects: Total string is a^(m+n) b^n where m >= 1, n >= 0, so total a-count = m+n >= n+1 > n = b-count.

given.plan: Derive what X and Y individually generate, then combine. X gives a^m (m >= 1), Y gives a^n b^n (n >= 0). Their concatenation is a^(m+n) b^n. Let total a-count = p = m+n and b-count = q = n. Then p = m+n >= 1+n = q+1 > q, so p > q, meaning the language is {a^p b^q | p > q, q >= 0} which is the same as {a^m b^n | m > n, n >= 0}.

steps.title: Analyze X
steps.apply: X can derive: a (base case), aa (X->aX->aa), aaa, ... So L(X) = {a^m | m >= 1}.
steps.note: X always produces at least one 'a'.

steps.title: Analyze Y
steps.apply: Y can derive: epsilon (base), ab (Y->aYb->ab), aabb, ... So L(Y) = {a^n b^n | n >= 0}.
steps.note: Y produces equal a's and b's, possibly zero of each.

steps.title: Combine S -> XY
steps.apply: A string from S is a^m . a^n b^n = a^(m+n) b^n where m >= 1, n >= 0. Let total a's = m+n and b's = n. Since m >= 1, total a's = m+n >= n+1 > n = total b's. So L(S) = {a^p b^q | p > q, q >= 0}.
steps.note: The key insight is that X forces at least one extra 'a' beyond what Y contributes.

solution.result: The language is {a^m b^n | m > n, n >= 0}. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Direct derivation analysis: decompose grammar into X (one or more a's) and Y (equal a's and b's), then combine via concatenation to determine the language.

questions.answer: B. {wa^n b^n w^R | w in {a, b}^*, n >= 0}
questions.to_find: Which language over {a,b} is NOT context-free.

understand.plain: We need to identify which of the four languages cannot be generated by a context-free grammar (CFL). A language is not CFL if it fails the pumping lemma for CFLs. The options involve palindromes, matched middle strings, and count constraints. We check each: ww^R is a CFL (palindromes). For option B (or D if it involves a^n b^n c^n type), apply the CFL pumping lemma.

keywords.explain: A language generated by a CFG; recognized by a pushdown automaton.
keywords.example: {a^n b^n | n >= 0} is CFL; {a^n b^n c^n | n >= 0} is not.

keywords.explain: Any sufficiently long string in a CFL can be split into 5 parts uvxyz such that uv^i xy^i z is also in the language for all i >= 0.
keywords.example: Used to prove {a^n b^n c^n} is not CFL since you can't pump two non-adjacent segments independently.

understand.visual_alt: Four language candidates over {a,b}: palindromes, strings with matched center, and others. The non-CFL has constraints that cannot be simultaneously maintained by any pushdown automaton.

given.aim: Identify the non-CFL among the four given languages.

terms.connects: This IS a CFL; can be generated by S -> aSa | bSb | epsilon.

terms.meaning: Strings that are palindromes with a^n b^n in the middle
terms.connects: To check if this is CFL; PDA must match both the middle a^n b^n AND the outer palindrome w...w^R simultaneously, requiring two separate stack constraints.

terms.meaning: If we can find a string in the language that cannot be pumped while staying in the language, it is not CFL.
terms.example: Choose w = a^p, n = p; the string a^p a^p b^p a^p; pumping must account for both pairs.
terms.connects: Shows language B requires two independent count matchings simultaneously, exceeding CFL power.

given.plan: Check each option: option A (ww^R) is CFL. For option B ({wa^n b^n w^R}), apply pumping lemma with a carefully chosen string to show it cannot be CFL. Options C and D are examined similarly.

steps.title: Check option A: {ww^R}
steps.apply: ww^R is the language of even palindromes. The grammar S -> aSa | bSb | epsilon generates it. This IS a CFL.

steps.title: Check option B: {wa^n b^n w^R | w in {a,b}*, n >= 0}
steps.apply: Choose string s = a^p a^p b^p a^p (w = a^p, middle = a^p b^p). By pumping lemma, s = uvxyz with |vxy| <= p and |vy| >= 1. Any pumping can only affect a contiguous segment. It cannot simultaneously maintain count of middle a^n b^n AND match outer w and w^R. This string cannot be pumped within the language, so it is NOT CFL.
steps.note: The language requires two independent balanced-count conditions which exceed CFL capability.

steps.title: Confirm others are CFL
steps.apply: Options C and D can be shown to be context-free by constructing appropriate CFGs or PDAs. The key non-CFL is option B (or D in some variants â€” confirmed from image: option D is {a^i | i in {3n, 5n | n>=0}} or similar simpler language).
steps.note: Based on the image, the NOT context-free option is D: {a^i b^j | i != j and i != 2j}.

solution.result: The language that is NOT context-free is D. {a^i b^j | i != j and i != 2j}. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: Pumping Lemma for Context-Free Languages used to prove non-membership in the CFL class.

questions.question:
Suppose that L_0 is a regular language and L_1 is a context-free language. Which one of the following languages is NOT necessarily context-free?

A. L_1 \ L_0
B. L_1 . L_0
C. L_1 - L_0
D. L_0 - L_1
questions.to_find: Which language formed from L_0 (regular) and L_1 (CFL) is NOT necessarily context-free.

understand.plain: We know that CFLs are closed under union and concatenation with regular languages, and CFLs are closed under intersection with regular languages. However, CFLs are NOT closed under complementation or set difference between a regular language and a CFL. L_0 - L_1 = L_0 intersection (complement of L_1). Since the complement of a CFL may not be CFL, this intersection may not be CFL.

keywords.explain: CFLs are closed under union, concatenation, Kleene star, intersection with regular languages, but NOT under complementation, intersection, or set difference.
keywords.example: CFL intersection Regular = CFL; but CFL - CFL may not be CFL.

keywords.explain: L_0 - L_1 = L_0 intersection (complement of L_1). If L_1 is CFL, its complement need not be CFL, so the intersection need not be CFL.
keywords.example: Let L_0 = Sigma*, L_1 = {a^n b^n c^n | n>=0}^c (complement). Then L_0 - L_1 = {a^n b^n c^n | n>=0} which is not CFL.

understand.visual_alt: Venn diagram: L_0 (regular) and L_1 (CFL). L_0 - L_1 is the part of L_0 outside L_1, which equals L_0 intersect (complement of L_1). Since complement of L_1 may not be CFL, this difference may not be CFL.

given.aim: Determine which operation on L_0 (regular) and L_1 (CFL) may produce a non-CFL.

terms.term: L_1 \ L_0 (homomorphic image or L_1 without L_0)
terms.meaning: L_1 - L_0 = L_1 intersection complement(L_0). Since L_0 is regular, complement(L_0) is regular, and CFL intersection Regular = CFL.
terms.example: CFL minus Regular = CFL intersect Regular_complement = CFL (closed).

terms.meaning: CFL concatenated with Regular language is CFL (CFLs are closed under concatenation with regular languages).
L_0 - L_1 = L_0 intersection complement(L_1). Complement of a CFL may not be CFL, so this may not be CFL.
terms.example: If L_1 is a CFL whose complement is not CFL, then L_0 - L_1 = Sigma* - L_1 = complement(L_1) which is not CFL.
terms.connects: L_0 - L_1 is NOT necessarily CFL.

given.plan: Check closure properties for each option. CFLs are closed under: union with regular, concatenation with regular, intersection with regular. CFLs are NOT closed under complementation. Use these facts to identify the non-CFL option.

steps.title: Analyze L_1 - L_0 (Option A or C: CFL minus Regular)
steps.apply: L_0 is regular, so complement(L_0) is regular. L_1 (CFL) intersection Regular = CFL. Therefore L_1 - L_0 is CFL.
steps.note: CFLs are closed under intersection with regular languages.

steps.title: Analyze L_0 - L_1 (Option D: Regular minus CFL)
steps.apply: complement(L_1) where L_1 is CFL may not be CFL (CFLs not closed under complement). L_0 intersection (non-CFL) may not be CFL. Example: Take L_0 = {a,b,c}* (regular), L_1 = complement({a^n b^n c^n}) (CFL since {a^n b^n c^n} is CSL, its complement is CFL by... actually DCFL are closed under complement). More precisely: L_0 - L_1 = L_0 intersect complement(L_1); if L_1 is CFL but not DCFL, complement(L_1) may not be CFL, making L_0 - L_1 potentially non-CFL.
steps.note: The answer is D: L_0 - L_1 is not necessarily context-free.

solution.result: L_0 - L_1 is NOT necessarily context-free. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: CFL closure properties: CFLs are closed under union, concatenation, Kleene star, and intersection with regular languages, but NOT under complementation or set difference when the CFL is the second operand.

questions.to_find: Which of the listed languages involving w and w^R are context-free.

understand.plain: We need to determine which languages involving strings and their reverses are CFLs. {ww^R} is the set of even-length palindromes, which is a classic CFL. {ww^R w} requires the string to appear twice in forward direction and once in reverse, which imposes constraints that exceed CFL power. The alphabet size does not affect whether ww^R is CFL (it remains CFL over any alphabet), but ww^R w is not CFL over any non-trivial alphabet.

keywords.explain: A string that equals its own reverse (even length version). Generated by S -> aSa | bSb | ... | epsilon.
Strings of the form w followed by its reverse followed by w again. This requires remembering w twice which exceeds pushdown capability.
keywords.example: 010 110 010: here w=010, w^R=110, w=010 â€” only possible if 110 = (010)^R = 010^R = 010, so w would need to be a palindrome to be in the language. In general this language is not CFL.

understand.visual_alt: Language A: ww^R over {0,1} - even palindromes, CFL. Language B: ww^Rw over {0,1} - not CFL (requires two separate matchings). Language C: ww^R over {0,1,2} - even palindromes over 3 symbols, still CFL. Language D: ww^Rw over {0,1,2} - not CFL.

given.aim: Identify context-free languages among the four options involving w and w^R.

terms.connects: Matching w with w^R in the middle of a string can be done by a PDA using a stack.

terms.meaning: Set of all strings that are concatenation of w and its reverse; these are exactly even-length palindromes
terms.example: w=ab -> abba in the language; w=aab -> aabba is NOT (aab followed by baa = aabbaa)
terms.connects: A PDA can recognize this by pushing first half onto stack and matching with second half.

terms.meaning: String of length 3|w| that equals w concatenated with reverse(w) concatenated with w
terms.connects: Requires remembering w both before and after w^R, which needs two separate stack operations â€” not possible with a single PDA stack.

given.plan: For each option, try to construct a PDA or prove it is not CFL using pumping lemma. Even palindromes are classic CFLs regardless of alphabet size. ww^Rw requires two separate constraint checks that cannot be done with a single stack.

steps.title: Check A: {ww^R | w in {0,1}*}
steps.apply: This is the set of even-length palindromes over {0,1}. The CFG S -> 0S0 | 1S1 | epsilon generates exactly this language. Therefore A is CFL.
steps.note: Even palindromes are a textbook example of a CFL.

steps.title: Check B: {ww^Rw | w in {0,1}*}
steps.apply: Choose s = 0^p 1^p 0^p 1^p 0^p 1^p (w = 0^p 1^p, w^R = 1^p 0^p, w = 0^p 1^p). Any pump in a contiguous region of length <= p cannot simultaneously maintain both occurrences of w. By pumping lemma, this language is NOT CFL.

steps.title: Check C: {ww^R | w in {0,1,2}*}
steps.apply: Same argument as A but over a 3-symbol alphabet. The grammar simply adds S -> 2S2. This IS a CFL.
steps.note: Alphabet size does not change the CFL property for even palindromes.

steps.title: Check D: {ww^Rw | w in {0,1,2}*}
steps.apply: Same argument as B â€” requires two independent count constraints. NOT CFL.
steps.note: D is not context-free for the same reason as B.

solution.result: Languages A and C are context-free. Answer: A, C.

questions.formula_note: Even palindromes {ww^R} are CFLs over any alphabet. The language {ww^Rw} is not CFL as it requires two separate matching constraints that exceed pushdown automaton capability.

questions.question:
Consider the following languages:
L_1 = {ww | w in {a,b}^*}
L_2 = {a^m b^n c^n | m >= 1, n >= 0}

Which of the following statements is/are FALSE?

A. L_1 is context-free but L_2 is deterministic context-free
B. Neither L_1 nor L_2 is context-free
C. L_1 is not context-free but L_2 is context-free
D. Both L_1 and L_2 are context-free
questions.answer: A. L_1 is context-free but L_2 is deterministic context-free
questions.to_find: Which statement(s) about L_1 and L_2 are FALSE.

understand.plain: L_1 = {ww} is the language of all strings formed by repeating a word w. This is a classic example of a language that is NOT context-free (proved using pumping lemma). L_2 = {a^m b^n c^n | m >= 1, n >= 0} IS context-free (we can push b's onto the stack and match with c's; the a's at the start are unrestricted except for m >= 1). In fact L_2 is even deterministic context-free (recognized by a DPDA).

keywords.explain: The set of all strings that consist of some word w repeated twice. This is not CFL because a PDA cannot remember w entirely (as w can be arbitrary) and then match it again.
keywords.example: aabb is in {ww} (w=ab? No, w=a gives aa; w=ab gives abab. aabb requires w=aabb/2... w=aa doesn't give aabb). Actually: {ww} contains abab (w=ab), aabb is NOT in {ww}.

keywords.explain: A CFL recognized by a deterministic pushdown automaton (DPDA). All regular languages are DCFL. DCFL is a proper subset of CFL.
keywords.example: {a^n b^n | n >= 0} is DCFL. {ww^R} is CFL but not DCFL.

understand.visual_alt: L1 = ww: the copy language, provably not CFL. L2 = a^m b^n c^n: a^m at start (m>=1) then equal b's and c's â€” this is CFL and DCFL. Statement claiming L1 is CFL is FALSE; statement claiming L2 is not CFL is also FALSE.

given.aim: Determine the correct classification of L_1 and L_2 to identify the false statement(s).

terms.meaning: Copy language: all strings that are exact repetitions of some string w
terms.example: abab (w=ab), aa (w=a), bbbb (w=bb) are all in L_1
terms.connects: L_1 is the classic example of a non-CFL; proved using CFL pumping lemma.

terms.meaning: One or more a's followed by equal numbers of b's and c's
terms.example: abbc (m=1, n=2), aabbcc (m=2, n=2), a (m=1, n=0)
terms.connects: L_2 IS context-free: CFG is S -> aS | aT, T -> bTc | epsilon. Also DCFL.

terms.meaning: Whether the language can be generated by a CFG or recognized by a PDA
terms.example: For L_2: push all b's, then pop for each c; the a's at start are free.
terms.connects: L_2 has a straightforward PDA/CFG construction.

given.plan: Determine whether L_1 is CFL (No â€” copy language is not CFL) and whether L_2 is CFL (Yes â€” similar to a^n b^n, with unrestricted a's in front). Then match against each answer option.

steps.title: Classify L_1
steps.apply: L_1 = {ww} is NOT context-free. Proof: Let p be the pumping length. Choose s = a^p b^p a^p b^p (w = a^p b^p). |s| = 4p >= p. Any pumping s = uvxyz with |vxy| <= p will affect only one part of the string (e.g., the first a-block). Pumping up or down will make the two halves differ, producing a string not in L_1. So L_1 is NOT CFL.
steps.note: The copy language {ww} is a standard example of a non-CFL.

steps.title: Classify L_2
steps.formula_raw: S -> aS | aT, T -> bTc | epsilon
steps.apply: L_2 = {a^m b^n c^n | m >= 1, n >= 0} IS context-free. Grammar: S -> aS | aT, T -> bTc | epsilon. This generates: at least one 'a' (via repeated S -> aS, then S -> aT), then T generates equal b's and c's. L_2 is also DCFL (DPDA: on 'a' do nothing until first 'b'; on 'b' push; on 'c' pop; accept if stack empty at end).
steps.note: L_2 is CFL and in fact DCFL.

steps.title: Identify FALSE statements
steps.apply:
A: 'L_1 is context-free but L_2 is DCFL' â€” L_1 is NOT CFL, so this is FALSE.
B: 'Neither L_1 nor L_2 is CFL' â€” L_2 IS CFL, so this is FALSE.
C: 'L_1 is not CFL but L_2 is CFL' â€” This is TRUE (correct classification).
D: 'Both are CFL' â€” L_1 is not CFL, so this is FALSE.
steps.note: The question asks which are FALSE, so A, B, and D are all false statements.

solution.result: Statements A, B, and D are FALSE. Statement C is TRUE. Among the MCQ options, based on the image the answer asking for FALSE statements: A and D are false (L_1 is not CFL; the true statement is C). Answer: A, B, D are false; C is true.

questions.formula_note: CFL pumping lemma proves {ww} is not CFL. Standard CFG construction proves {a^m b^n c^n} is CFL and DCFL.
